jokes.text: Shit. I just opened up the old cut.
jokes.setup: Frank dramatically forces his way through an unlocked door
jokes.punchline: He immediately injures himself on entry
jokes.explanation: Classic Frank physical comedy - he creates drama where none exists and immediately pays for it. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Shit. I just opened up the old cut.

jokes.text: Why didn't you open the door? It wasn't locked.
jokes.setup: Frank just dramatically broke in and injured himself
jokes.punchline: The door was unlocked the whole time
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/payoff revealing Frank's unnecessary drama. Excellent economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Why didn't you open the door?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It wasn't locked.

jokes.text: Frank using Mac's sock and newspaper to stuff his wound
jokes.setup: Frank needs to stop bleeding from his injury
jokes.punchline: He uses whatever's handy in Mac's apartment in increasingly unsanitary ways
jokes.explanation: Gross-out visual comedy that establishes Frank's disgusting pragmatism and sets up the wound running gag.

dialogue.line: Frank using Mac's sock and newspaper to stuff his wound

jokes.text: I drew the short straw, so here I am.
jokes.setup: Frank is supposedly helping Mac with gay pride
jokes.punchline: He admits he was forced into it and doesn't want to be there
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Frank's reluctance and the gang's lack of genuine support for Mac.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I drew the short straw, so here I am.

jokes.text: And, to be honest, now that you're gay, I get you even less.
jokes.setup: Frank admits he never understood Mac
jokes.punchline: Being gay makes Mac even more incomprehensible to Frank
jokes.explanation: Great Frank honesty - he's trying to be supportive but can't help being Frank. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: And, to be honest, now that you're gay, I get you even less.

jokes.text: Frank, you're a 75-year-old man with a face that looks like hamburger meat. I'm sure you're safe.
jokes.setup: Frank worries about gay men hitting on him
jokes.punchline: Mac brutally assesses Frank's attractiveness level
jokes.explanation: Perfectly brutal insult with great visual imagery. 'Hamburger meat' is a memorably specific and harsh comparison.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Frank, you're a 75-year-old man with a face that looks like hamburger meat. I'm sure you're safe.

jokes.text: This is a much better spread than they have at the straight orgies.
jokes.setup: Frank and Mac are at a buffet at a leather bar
jokes.punchline: Frank casually compares it to straight orgy catering
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank line - casually revealing disturbing information while focusing on food quality. Excellent character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: This is a much better spread than they have at the straight orgies.

jokes.text: Ooh, there must be some hot sauce on them wings. They're buffalo wings.
jokes.setup: Frank tastes the chicken he just contaminated with his blood
jokes.punchline: He thinks his own blood is hot sauce, then realizes it's just buffalo sauce
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment - Frank's complete disconnect from reality as he eats contaminated food.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Ooh, there must be some hot sauce on them wings. They're buffalo wings.

jokes.text: Mac, unzip this thing, so we can find out what it wants.
jokes.setup: A person in leather gear approaches them
jokes.punchline: Frank treats the person like an alien creature to be studied
jokes.explanation: Great Frank confusion treating a leather mask like incomprehensible alien technology.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Mac, unzip this thing, so we can find out what it wants.

jokes.text: Why? Because I'm straight? No. Because you put your nose rags in the chicken.
jokes.setup: Frank assumes discrimination when asked to leave
jokes.punchline: He's being kicked out for contaminating food, not homophobia
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Frank's victim complex versus the very reasonable complaint about food contamination.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Why? Because I'm straight? No. Because you put your nose rags in the chicken.

jokes.text: Hey, how much I got to pay you to let my boy Mac here take you for a spin? I'm not a prostitute. You do this for fun?
jokes.setup: Frank tries to hire someone for Mac
jokes.punchline: His confusion that someone would dress in leather without payment
jokes.explanation: Frank's transactional view of sexuality creates awkward moment that reveals his fundamental misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Hey, how much I got to pay you to let my boy Mac here take you for a spin?

dialogue.speaker: Leather Man
dialogue.line: I'm not a prostitute.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You do this for fun?

jokes.text: I mean, one false move and these fairies could poke me full of holes. 'Fairies'? What year is it in your head?
jokes.setup: Frank uses outdated terminology for gay men
jokes.punchline: Mac calls out how anachronistic Frank's language is
jokes.explanation: Good character moment highlighting Frank's outdated worldview, with Mac as the voice of reason for once.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I mean, one false move and these fairies could poke me full of holes.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: 'Fairies'? What year is it in your head?

jokes.text: When you came out to your father, did you come out as a top or a bottom? A boy or a girl?
jokes.setup: Frank tries to understand Mac's sexuality
jokes.punchline: His complete misunderstanding of basic gay terminology and concepts
jokes.explanation: Frank's ignorance creates cringe comedy, though it's somewhat predictable territory for the character.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: When you came out to your father, did you come out as a top or a bottom? A boy or a girl?

jokes.text: You're gay and you're dancing with a hot chick who is God? Yes. The Catholics really fucked you up.
jokes.setup: Mac describes his confused spiritual/sexual metaphor
jokes.punchline: Frank correctly diagnoses the source of Mac's confusion
jokes.explanation: Excellent Frank moment - for once he's perceptive about psychological cause of Mac's issues. Great economy and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You're gay and you're dancing with a hot chick who is God?

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: The Catholics really fucked you up.

jokes.text: Frank's nose swelling and him trying to fix it with insulation
jokes.setup: Frank's injury is getting worse throughout the episode
jokes.punchline: He uses increasingly inappropriate materials to treat a serious medical issue
jokes.explanation: Running gag escalation showing Frank's terrible self-care and stubborn refusal to seek proper medical help.

dialogue.line: Frank's nose swelling and him trying to fix it with insulation

jokes.text: My cellmate ratted on me for having an extra pillow. I cut out his tongue with a rusty pair of pliers and fed it to the maggots.
jokes.setup: Mac's dad casually mentions minor prison complaint
jokes.punchline: Massively disproportionate and graphic revenge for trivial offense
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment establishing Luther's psychotic nature while treating extreme violence as casual conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Luther
dialogue.line: My cellmate ratted on me for having an extra pillow. I cut out his tongue with a rusty pair of pliers and fed it to the maggots.

jokes.text: Cool... Very cool.
jokes.setup: Luther just described graphic torture and murder
jokes.punchline: Mac responds with awkward enthusiasm trying to bond with his father
jokes.explanation: Perfect uncomfortable reaction beat - Mac's desperate attempt to connect leads to endorsing violence. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Cool... Very cool.

jokes.text: My son... finally knocked someone up. I'm gonna be a grandfather.
jokes.setup: Mac tries to come out using abstract metaphors about storms and angels
jokes.punchline: Luther completely misunderstands and thinks Mac got someone pregnant
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing how Mac's poetic coming-out attempt backfired completely due to his confusing metaphors.

dialogue.speaker: Luther
dialogue.line: My son... finally knocked someone up. I'm gonna be a grandfather.

jokes.text: My son... finally knocked someone up. I'm gonna be a grandfather.
jokes.setup: Mac's metaphorical explanation of his internal struggle
jokes.punchline: His father completely misinterpreting it as news of pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - Mac's coming out attempt gets completely reframed as heterosexual success

dialogue.speaker: Mac's Dad
dialogue.line: My son... finally knocked someone up. I'm gonna be a grandfather.

jokes.text: I never really got you, you know? But now, I see that there's hope for you yet. Well, I-I mean, not with your life, but with the life of your son.
jokes.setup: Luther expresses newfound pride in Mac
jokes.punchline: He immediately clarifies he still thinks Mac is hopeless personally
jokes.explanation: Perfect Luther cruelty - giving Mac hope then immediately crushing it while maintaining the misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Luther
dialogue.line: I never really got you, you know? But now, I see that there's hope for you yet. Well, I-I mean, not with your life, but with the life of your son.

jokes.text: Hey, if it's not a boy, you flush that shit out and try again.
jokes.setup: Luther assumes Mac's having a baby
jokes.punchline: Casually advocates abortion if it's not male
jokes.explanation: Dark character comedy showing Luther's misogyny and violent casualness about reproductive choices.

dialogue.speaker: Luther
dialogue.line: Hey, if it's not a boy, you flush that shit out and try again.

jokes.text: Yeah. Yeah, definitely. I'll flush that shit out, and you... I'm gonna give you a boy, Dad.
jokes.setup: Mac desperately wants his father's approval
jokes.punchline: He agrees to horrible things just to maintain the misunderstanding and keep Luther happy
jokes.explanation: Cringe moment showing Mac's willingness to betray his values for paternal approval, though somewhat predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah. Yeah, definitely. I'll flush that shit out, and you... I'm gonna give you a boy, Dad.

jokes.text: Frank's reaction to seeing Mac in bed with a woman
jokes.setup: Frank went to get Mac for the parade
jokes.punchline: Mac is apparently trying to get a woman pregnant to please his father
jokes.explanation: Visual callback to Mac's misguided attempt to fulfill his father's expectations, setting up Frank's confrontation.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What the hell are you doing?

jokes.text: What are you gonna have you, me, dancing on top of the gay float? No. No, no, the press'll murder us.
jokes.setup: They need someone to dance on the pride float
jokes.punchline: The straight characters are horrified at the idea of being seen as fake gay allies
jokes.explanation: Good satire of performative allyship and fear of being called out for insincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What are you gonna have you, me, dancing on top of the gay float? No. No, no, the press'll murder us.

jokes.text: Allow me to introduce Paddy's top gay, a man everybody in the neighborhood knows, everybody loves. The gay that will rope in all the gays. Cricket.
jokes.setup: Frank promises to deliver a replacement gay man for the parade
jokes.punchline: He presents Cricket, who is clearly not gay, attractive, or representative of anything positive
jokes.explanation: Great ironic buildup with Frank's marketing speak followed by the reveal of disaster Cricket as the solution.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Allow me to introduce Paddy's top gay, a man everybody in the neighborhood knows, everybody loves. The gay that will rope in all the gays. Cricket.

jokes.text: He looks like a monster, and you look like a monster. We're not trying to invite a bar full of monster men.
jokes.setup: The gang rejects Cricket as their parade representative
jokes.punchline: Dennis brutally insults both Cricket and Frank's appearance
jokes.explanation: Dennis's vanity and cruelty on display, with good economy in dismissing multiple people as monsters.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: He looks like a monster, and you look like a monster. We're not trying to invite a bar full of monster men.

jokes.text: Frank, it's 2018, and you're the only person who doesn't seem to understand gay culture.
jokes.setup: Frank has consistently misunderstood gay culture all episode
jokes.punchline: Charlie, of all people, calls out Frank's ignorance
jokes.explanation: Good role reversal with Charlie being the voice of modern reason while Frank remains stuck in the past.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Frank, it's 2018, and you're the only person who doesn't seem to understand gay culture.

jokes.text: Lady, I'm not going anywhere until I see an Abraham Lincoln. Here you go. What is this? Like, 73 cents. No. I-I said Abraham Lincoln. Those are pennies.
jokes.setup: Cricket demands payment with an Abraham Lincoln
jokes.punchline: The gang gives him pennies since Lincoln is on them, Cricket accepts this logic
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay exploitation showing both Cricket's desperation and the gang's cheapness.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: Lady, I'm not going anywhere until I see an Abraham Lincoln.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Here you go.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: What is this? Like, 73 cents. No. I-I said Abraham Lincoln.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Those are pennies.

jokes.text: No, no, no, no. Lady, I'm not going anywhere until I see an Abraham Lincoln.
jokes.setup: Cricket is being paid for his participation
jokes.punchline: He demands to see a five-dollar bill using archaic slang
jokes.explanation: Cricket's street terminology and specific demands despite his desperation. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: No, no, no, no. Lady, I'm not going anywhere until I see an Abraham Lincoln.

jokes.text: Like, 73 cents. No. I-I said Abraham Lincoln. There's a bunch of Lincolns in there. Those are pennies.
jokes.setup: Cricket wants to see an 'Abraham Lincoln'
jokes.punchline: Dennis gives him pennies, which technically have Lincoln but aren't what Cricket meant
jokes.explanation: Dennis's pedantic literal interpretation vs Cricket's street slang. Good wordplay setup.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: Like, 73 cents. No. I-I said Abraham Lincoln.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: There's a bunch of Lincolns in there. Those are pennies.

jokes.text: Frank, it's 2018, and you're the only person who doesn't seem to understand gay culture.
jokes.setup: Frank's continued confusion about gay culture
jokes.punchline: Dennis pointing out Frank's anachronistic worldview
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on Frank's outdated attitudes, concise observation

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Frank, it's 2018, and you're the only person who doesn't seem to understand gay culture.

jokes.text: Sometimes you got to let the blood flow in order to start the healing. Some cuts you just can't plug up.
jokes.setup: Frank has been trying to plug his wound all episode
jokes.punchline: He uses his medical situation as a metaphor for Mac's emotional state
jokes.explanation: Surprisingly profound Frank moment where he connects his physical wound to Mac's emotional struggle. Good character growth and metaphorical depth.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Sometimes you got to let the blood flow in order to start the healing. Some cuts you just can't plug up.

jokes.text: They think they're seeing a Blake Shelton concert, and I put your dad in the front row.
jokes.setup: Frank has arranged for Mac to perform for his father
jokes.punchline: He lied to prisoners about what they're seeing and specifically positioned Luther
jokes.explanation: Good Frank scheming showing his bizarre but effective problem-solving and setup for the climactic performance.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: They think they're seeing a Blake Shelton concert, and I put your dad in the front row.

jokes.text: Unfortunately... Blake Shelton will not be making it today. But, he did send me in his stead, and I've got something special to show you.
jokes.setup: Prisoners expecting a Blake Shelton concert
jokes.punchline: Mac has to explain the bait-and-switch before his emotional performance
jokes.explanation: Setup for the dramatic dance sequence, functional but not particularly funny on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Unfortunately... Blake Shelton will not be making it today. But, he did send me in his stead, and I've got something special to show you.

jokes.text: I named him that.
jokes.setup: Mac introduces himself as Ronald McDonald
jokes.punchline: Frank takes credit for naming Mac after the clown
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank interjection showing his pride in terrible parenting decisions, excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I named him that.

jokes.text: My name is Ronald McDonald, and... I named him that.
jokes.setup: Mac introduces himself by his full name
jokes.punchline: His father proudly takes credit for the McDonald's mascot name
jokes.explanation: Mac's dad's misplaced pride in his son's unfortunate name. Good character beat before the emotional climax.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: My name is Ronald McDonald, and...

dialogue.speaker: Mac's Dad
dialogue.line: I named him that.

jokes.text: My God. I get it.
jokes.setup: Luther watches Mac's emotional dance performance about his inner struggle
jokes.punchline: The hardened criminal is moved to tears and understanding
jokes.explanation: Powerful emotional payoff - the violent psychopath understands Mac through art where words failed. Great economy and earned moment.

dialogue.speaker: Luther
dialogue.line: My God. I get it.
